description: This schematic illustrates the ordered precipitates in microstructures of two duplex low-density Fe-Mn-Al-C steels (5 wt.% Ni-containing and Ni-free) under different annealing temperatures. The upper part shows the micro-scale microstructure: γ austenite matrix, grain boundary (GB) B2 precipitates, B2 stringer bands formed in Ni-containing steel annealed at 500-700℃, and α + nano-sized B2 phase region formed at 900-1050℃. Two lower nano-scale magnified regions are presented: the red region demonstrates nano-sized blocky κ-carbides distributed in γ matrix, and the blue region shows nano-sized B2 particles precipitated in α ferrite matrix. It summarizes that B2 forms as micron-sized particles, GB precipitates and nano-discs in ferrite at 900-1050℃, while κ-carbides form as nano-sized precipitates in austenite at 500-700℃, with α phase in Ni-containing steel replaced by B2 stringer bands at low annealing temperatures. This figure clarifies the size, distribution and composition of ordered precipitates under different thermal conditions, supporting the analysis of mechanical property differences between the two steels.

This figure illustrates the microstructural evolution of Ni-containing Fe-Mn-Al-C low-density steel under varied isothermal annealing temperatures, combining scanning electron microscopy (SEM) for micro-scale observation and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) for nano-scale characterization. Subfigures a and b show the 500℃ annealed microstructure, consisting of B2 stringer bands, γ + κ matrix, and grain boundary B2 (GB-B2) precipitates. Subfigures c and d present similar micro-scale structure after 700℃ annealing, with sustained B2 stringer bands and γ + κ phases. Subfigures e and f are TEM bright-field images of high-temperature (900-1050℃) annealed samples: e displays nano-disk B2 precipitates in α matrix, and f reveals cuboid κ carbides uniformly distributed in γ matrix. The results validate that annealing temperature dominates phase morphology, distribution and type, supporting the correlation between heat treatment parameters and microstructure of low-density steels; Ni-free steels follow a similar trend except forming needle-like κ carbides in α phase at low temperatures instead of B2 stringer bands.

This is a scanning electron microscopy (SEM) image displaying arrays of regular triangular nano-indentation marks on the microstructure of Fe-Mn-Al-C lightweight steels (both Ni-containing and Ni-free variants studied in the work), with a 2 μm scale bar provided for size reference. The indentation rows are deliberately placed across distinct phase regions to measure the nano-hardness of individual phases and combined phase assemblies. As reported in the associated text, the nano-indentation tests were performed at a loading/unloading rate of 500 μN/s up to a maximum load of 10000 μN with a 20 s hold time, with at least 25 repeated measurements per targeted phase region. Phases characterized include γ austenite, α ferrite, B2 intermetallics (stringer bands, micro-sized particles, nano-discs), κ-carbides, and combined phase regions where individual phase hardness cannot be isolated. This figure validates the phase-resolved nano-hardness measurement approach used to quantify the contribution of each phase to the overall mechanical properties of the lightweight steels, supporting the core conclusion that phase composition, morphology, and interatomic bonding strength govern the material's hardness performance.
